statement.title: Statement
statement.content:
Description
You are given two non-negative integers $ x $ , $ y $ . Find two non-negative integers $ p $ and $ q $ such that $ p\;\&\;q=0 $ , and $ |x-p|+|y-q| $ is minimized. Here, $ \& $ denotes the [bitwise AND operation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bitwise_operation#AND).

Input Format
Each test contains multiple test cases. The first line contains the number of test cases $ t $ ( $ 1 \le t \le 10^4 $ ). The description of the test cases follows.

The only line of each test case contains two non-negative integers $ x $ and $ y $ ( $ 0 \le x,y < 2^{30} $ ).

Output Format
For each test case, output two non-negative integers $ p $ and $ q $ you found. If there are multiple pairs of $ p $ and $ q $ satisfying the conditions, you may output any of them.

It can be proven that under the constraints of the problem, any valid solution satisfies $ \max(p,q)<2^{31} $ .

Examples
Sample 1
Input
7
0 0
1 1
3 6
7 11
4 4
123 321
1073741823 1073741822
Output
0 0
2 1
3 8
6 9
4 3
128 321
1073741824 1073741822

Note
In the first test case, one valid pair would be $ p=0 $ and $ q=0 $ , as $ 0\,\&\,0=0 $ and $ |x-p|+|y-q|=|0-0|+|0-0|=0 $ is the minimum over all pairs of $ p $ and $ q $ .

In the third test case, one valid pair would be $ p=3 $ and $ q=8 $ , as $ 3\,\&\,8=0 $ and $ |x-p|+|y-q|=|3-3|+|8-6|=2 $ is the minimum over all pairs of $ p $ and $ q $ . Note that $ (p,q)=(3,4) $ is also a valid pair.

solutions.title: Solution
solutions.content:
2187B - Shortest Statement Ever
This is the intended solution.
Find the highest bit $$$b$$$ such that both $$$p$$$ and $$$q$$$ have a $$$1$$$. We need to modify $$$p$$$ and $$$q$$$ such that one of $$$p$$$ or $$$q$$$ has a $$$0$$$ on this bit. Without loss of generality, there are two ways to do this:
Increment $$$p$$$ until this bit is $$$0$$$, and let $$$y=q$$$. In this case, if $$$x\,\&\,y=0$$$, update the answer.
Let $$$x=\underbrace{..}_{\text{high bits same as }p}1\underbrace{00..000}_{b-1 \text{ zeroes}}$$$, and $$$y=\underbrace{..}_{\text{high bits same as }q}0\underbrace{11..111}_{b-1 \text{ ones}}$$$. It can be shown that $$$x\,\&\,y=0$$$.
It can be proven that this greedy leads to an optimal solution. Overall complexity $$$O(1)$$$ per test case.
There is another interesting fact: there always exists a solution where $$$p=x$$$ or $$$q=y$$$. You may also build a greedy solution on top of this observation.
There is also a dynamic programming solution, which is significantly more tedious.
